original: Psalm 42:7: deep calls unto deep in the sound of waters.
english: Depth does not answer surface; it calls to what is already deep enough to hear.
spanish: La profundidad no responde a la superficie; llama a lo que ya es bastante hondo para oír.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm Hebrew/KJV/Spanish witness alignment before promotion. Short paraphrase only.

original: Psalm 46:10: stillness is joined to knowing.
english: Knowledge begins where agitation stops performing itself.
spanish: El conocimiento empieza donde la agitación deja de representarse a sí misma.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm verse rendering before promotion.

original: Psalm 119:105: the word is a lamp for the feet and light for the path.
english: Guidance is not a map of the world; it is enough light for the next step.
spanish: La guía no es un mapa del mundo; es suficiente luz para el siguiente paso.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm canonical locator before promotion.

original: Psalm 130:1: the cry rises out of the depths.
english: Prayer starts where the voice admits the depth it is speaking from.
spanish: La oración empieza donde la voz admite la hondura desde la que habla.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm verse rendering before promotion.

original: Rigveda 1.1 opens with Agni as priestly fire.
english: The first hymn begins by making fire the mediator between offering and world.
spanish: El primer himno empieza haciendo del fuego el mediador entre ofrenda y mundo.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm Sanskrit verse and translation witness before promotion.

original: Rigveda 10.129 asks what existed before being and non-being.
english: Cosmogony becomes philosophy when it doubts even the first distinction.
spanish: La cosmogonía se vuelve filosofía cuando duda incluso de la primera distinción.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm hymn locator before promotion.

original: Rigveda 10.90 presents Purusha as a cosmic body from which orders unfold.
english: The world is imagined as dismemberment: order born from a body too vast to remain whole.
spanish: El mundo se imagina como desmembramiento: orden nacido de un cuerpo demasiado vasto para permanecer entero.
notes: Real capture from public web source. Review-needed: confirm verse selection before promotion.

original: Mundaka Upanishad gives the image of two birds on one tree.
english: One self eats the fruit; the other watches from the same branch.
spanish: Un yo come el fruto; el otro observa desde la misma rama.
notes: Real capture from public-domain web witness. Review-needed: confirm section and Sanskrit witness before promotion.

artifact: Katha Upanishad — Chariot of the Self
original: Katha Upanishad uses the chariot as an image of body, senses, mind, and self.
english: The person is a moving assembly: driver, reins, horses, road, and witness.
spanish: La persona es un ensamblaje en movimiento: conductor, riendas, caballos, camino y testigo.
notes: Real capture from public-domain web witness. Review-needed: confirm verse locator before promotion.

original: Isha Upanishad tradition frames fullness as remaining full even after fullness comes forth.
english: The source does not diminish by overflowing into what it makes.
spanish: La fuente no disminuye al desbordarse en aquello que produce.
notes: Real capture from public-domain web witness. Review-needed: confirm textual witness before promotion.

original: Masnavi tells of people touching different parts of an elephant in darkness.
english: Partial contact becomes false certainty when the lamp is missing.
spanish: El contacto parcial se vuelve falsa certeza cuando falta la lámpara.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi
notes: Real capture from public-domain web witness. Review-needed: confirm book/story locator before promotion.

original: Masnavi tells of a grammarian whose knowledge fails before the river.
english: A theory of swimming cannot save the one who never entered the water.
spanish: Una teoría de la natación no salva a quien nunca entró al agua.
recommendedEditionSource: E. H. Whinfield — Masnavi i Ma'navi
notes: Real capture from public-domain web witness. Review-needed: confirm book/story locator before promotion.

original: Greek Anthology epitaphs often let the tombstone speak to the passerby.
english: The stone turns death into address: stop, read, remember, continue.
spanish: La piedra convierte la muerte en llamada: detente, lee, recuerda, sigue.
recommendedEditionSource: W. R. Paton — Greek Anthology, Volume V
notes: Real capture from public-domain digitized witness. Review-needed: select exact epigram locator before promotion.

original: Greek Anthology epitaphs frequently preserve the dead lost at sea.
english: When there is no body to bury, the poem becomes the shore.
spanish: Cuando no hay cuerpo que enterrar, el poema se vuelve orilla.
recommendedEditionSource: W. R. Paton — Greek Anthology, Volume V
